topic: Area of Study 7: Art Music since 1910
subject: music

items.correct: No - atonal music avoids a key centre but is often rigorously organised; serial works order all twelve pitches in a strict row.
items.misconception: Atonal means the composer chose notes at random with no system.
items.random_wrong: Atonal music is music performed without any rehearsal.
items.why: Students equate the absence of tonality with the absence of order, yet Schoenberg developed serialism precisely to give atonal music a systematic structure.

items.q: Does minimalism mean that almost nothing happens in the music?
items.correct: No - minimalist works change continuously through gradual processes such as phasing and additive rhythm built on repeating patterns.
items.misconception: Minimalist music just repeats one idea identically with no development.
items.random_wrong: Minimalist music uses only one instrument playing one note.
items.why: The style's surface repetition hides constant micro-change; hearing the gradual process is the key to analysing Reich and Glass.

items.q: Is 'serialism' just another word for any dissonant modern music?
items.correct: No - serialism is a specific method ordering the twelve chromatic pitches into a row used in prime, inversion, retrograde and retrograde-inversion forms.
items.misconception: Any harsh twentieth-century piece can be called serialist.
items.random_wrong: Serialism means writing music for television serials.
items.why: Serialism names a compositional technique, not a sound; much dissonant music since 1910 (Stravinsky's primitivism, spectralism) uses entirely different systems.
Correct answer: Schoenberg. “Stravinsky” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which composer is most associated with minimalist works such as 'Music for 18 Musicians'?
items.why: Correct answer: Reich. “Glass” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What distinguishes Classical (1750-1820) from Baroque style?
items.correct: Clearer melodic phrases, homophonic texture, balanced structures like sonata form.
items.random_wrong: Classical means any old serious music.
items.why: Correct answer: Clearer melodic phrases, homophonic texture, balanced structures like sonata form.. “A piece six bars long.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What does the time signature 6/8 indicate?
items.correct: Two beats per bar, each beat divided into three (compound duple).
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Two beats per bar, each beat divided into three (compound duple).. “Three notes played at once.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which of these is the best definition of “Minimalism”?
items.correct: Style emerging in the 1960s using repetition and gradual process, exemplified by Steve Reich and Philip Glass.
items.misconception: Compositional method using a fixed ordering of all twelve chromatic pitches, developed by Schoenberg around 1923.
items.random_wrong: Music incorporating chance elements, associated with John Cage's '4'33"' (1952) and Lutoslawski.
items.why: Correct answer: Style emerging in the 1960s using repetition and gradual process, exemplified by Steve Reich and Philip Glass.. “Compositional method using a fixed ordering of all twelve chromatic pitches, developed by Schoenberg around 1923.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of these is the best definition of “Spectralism”?
items.correct: Late twentieth-century approach analysing the acoustic spectrum of sound, developed by Grisey and Murail in 1970s France.
items.misconception: Style emerging in the 1960s using repetition and gradual process, exemplified by Steve Reich and Philip Glass.
items.random_wrong: Style using recorded environmental and instrumental sounds as raw material, founded by Pierre Schaeffer in 1948.
items.why: Correct answer: Late twentieth-century approach analysing the acoustic spectrum of sound, developed by Grisey and Murail in 1970s France.. “Style emerging in the 1960s using repetition and gradual process, exemplified by Steve Reich and Philip Glass.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of these is the best definition of “Atonality”?
items.correct: Music without a tonal centre, pioneered by Schoenberg around 1908 in works such as the Three Piano Pieces Op. 11.
items.misconception: Music incorporating chance elements, associated with John Cage's '4'33"' (1952) and Lutoslawski.
items.random_wrong: Style using recorded environmental and instrumental sounds as raw material, founded by Pierre Schaeffer in 1948.
items.why: It's Music without a tonal centre, pioneered by Schoenberg around 1908 in works such as the Three Piano Pieces Op. 11.. “Music incorporating chance elements, associated with John Cage's '4'33"' (1952) and Lutoslawski.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which of these is the best definition of “Aleatoric music”?
items.correct: Music incorporating chance elements, associated with John Cage's '4'33"' (1952) and Lutoslawski.
items.misconception: Music without a tonal centre, pioneered by Schoenberg around 1908 in works such as the Three Piano Pieces Op. 11.
items.random_wrong: Late twentieth-century approach analysing the acoustic spectrum of sound, developed by Grisey and Murail in 1970s France.
items.why: It's Music incorporating chance elements, associated with John Cage's '4'33"' (1952) and Lutoslawski.. “Music without a tonal centre, pioneered by Schoenberg around 1908 in works such as the Three Piano Pieces Op. 11.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did John Cage compose his silent work '4'33"'?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1952. “1968” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
